05
Product Teardown · Case Study 05 · May 2026

OZi — Quick commerce. Slow decisions.

OZi is building quick commerce for parents and kids in India — Blume Ventures backed, 0→1 stage. The delivery is fast. The decision is not. This is the activation gap hiding inside a brilliant product.

Company OZi · Blume Ventures
Stage 0→1 · Series A Backed
Research Primary app usage + competitive analysis
Frameworks JTBD · KANO · ICE · 5 Whys · PRD Thinking
01 Company Context

The bet
OZi made.

OZi is building what no kids commerce platform in India has nailed — quick commerce for parents. Not just fast delivery but a trusted, curated destination for everything a child needs. Blume Ventures backed, founded by a team with IIM Bangalore, ex-OYO, ex-BCG, ex-Amazon pedigree. They are 0→1. The product works. The activation doesn't.

BV
Blume Ventures
Backed by one of India's most active early-stage VCs
0→1
Product Stage
Founder: IIM Bangalore, ex-OYO. Head of Product: ex-BCG, Cars24
~74
Mins Delivery
Speed of delivery is solved. Speed of decision is not.
1
Core Gap
No guided discovery for parents who don't know what to buy
Jobs To Be Done
Parents are not hiring OZi to browse a catalogue. They are hiring it to make the right decision for their child — fast, confidently, without regret. The catalogue is there. The confidence is not.
02 User Research & Evidence

What the app
actually feels like.

Research method: Primary — 30 minutes of personal app usage, full purchase flow from home to checkout. Comparative — FirstCry usage for same journey. Product analysis across category structure, filter logic, image quality, and trust signals.

Transparency: This is primary research with no Play Store review data available at scale. Observations are first-hand. Competitor comparison is against FirstCry — the dominant incumbent.
What works
Home page UX — warm, child-friendly, rounded, pastel. Visual language is strong.
Category organisation — Infant Fashion, Occasion Wear, Toys all logically structured.
Delivery speed — 74 minute delivery is competitive and real.
Filter variety — Gender, Age, Occasion, Closure Type, Material all available.
Try & Buy badge — good trust signal on product cards.
What doesn't
Product image quality — low resolution on click-through pages undermines premium feel.
Filter state resets between sub-categories — Boy filter in Party Wear disappears on switching to Dungarees.
Single-select gender filter — can't select Boy + Unisex simultaneously for a parent buying for siblings.
No guided discovery — 93 products in Infant Fashion with no "help me choose" path for unsure parents.
Occasion Wear has no occasion filter — only segments by age group inside it.
— Primary User Segment
Primary — Dual-Income Urban Parent
Both partners working, metro city, likely IT or corporate sector. High purchase intent, low time and patience for browsing without guidance. Shops on mobile during commute or lunch break. Needs confidence quickly.
Secondary — First-Time Parent
New to buying for a child, unsure about sizing, occasions, trends. Needs hand-holding through the decision. Would benefit enormously from AI-guided discovery and virtual try-on.
Trade-off on segments: We are not designing for the full-time parent at home who has time to go to market and see for themselves. They have offline access and don't need the guidance layer urgently. Our focus is the time-poor urban working parent for whom OZi's entire value proposition is speed AND confidence.
03 Problem Definition

One sentence
explains the drop-off.

Synthesised Problem Statement

A working parent, shopping for their child's upcoming occasion, downloads OZi looking for quick curated options that will make their kid stand out — trusting the app to guide them to the right choice fast.

Instead they land in a catalogue of 93 products with no occasion filter, no guidance on trend or fit, and no AI or human help to bridge the gap — leaving them more confused than when they started. They think of going offline to buy. This costs OZi the activation and conversion not because the parent lacked intent but because the product lacked intelligence.

Why 1
Parents drop off without purchasing despite high intent.
Why 2
Because after seeing multiple products they can't determine which is the right fit for the occasion — they liked more than one and couldn't reach a confident decision.
Why 3
Because the app shows no trend context, occasion guidance, or style curation — there is no signal for what is currently popular or appropriate for the specific occasion.
Why 4
Because OZi has no intelligent layer that understands the parent's specific need — occasion, age, gender, trend — and narrows the catalogue to a confident recommendation.
Why 5
Because the product was built as a catalogue, not as an advisor. Offline, a shopkeeper picks from the whole lot and guides the decision. OZi has the inventory but not the intelligence.
"OZi is a quick commerce platform with a slow commerce problem — it delivers speed of delivery but not speed of decision. The shopkeeper is missing from the digital experience."
04 KANO Prioritisation

What to fix first
and why.

OZi is a 0→1 product. KANO principle: fix all Must Haves before any Performance feature. No Delighters until the foundation is solid.

Must Have — Fix First
Image quality on product pages — trust breaks without premium feel
Filter persistence across sub-categories — basic UX expectation
AI Guided Discovery — core conversion mechanism, the shopkeeper replacement
Performance — Fix After
Multi-select gender filter — improves browsing for parents with multiple kids
Delighters — Later
Virtual Try-On — see outfit on your child before buying
Community — ask other parents, get parenting advice (Phase 2)
— User Stories
1
MH · Image Quality
As a concerned parent excited about buying for my child, I want high-quality images that convey a premium feel, so that I feel confident I am buying something of real value and not something that will disappoint on the occasion.
2
MH · Filter
As a working parent browsing on the go, I want my filters to stay applied as I move between sub-categories, so that I can browse the right segment fast without repeating the same selections every time.
3
MH · AI Discovery
As a dual-income urban parent used to a shopkeeper guiding decisions, I want an AI advisor that understands the occasion, age, and current trends and narrows the catalogue to a confident recommendation, so that I buy the right thing fast without the uncertainty of merely guessing and hoping it looks good on the day.
4
Perf · Gender Filter
As a parent of a boy with a younger sister, I want to select Boy and Unisex simultaneously in filters, so that I can find clothing that works primarily for my son but can also be worn by his sister — maximising value without two separate searches.
5
Delighter · Try-On
As a first-time parent unsure about committing to a purchase, I want to see how the outfit looks on my child before buying, so that I get the confidence of an offline shopping experience from home — without the uncertainty of guessing until delivery arrives.
05 Solution Framework

Five interventions,
one direction.

1
Must HaveImage & Trust
Premium Product Imagery
Collaborate with designers and developers to upgrade product photography — vibrant colours, clean lighting, smooth textures that convey quality. The goal is not to copy FirstCry's clinical style but to match OZi's warm visual language from the home page all the way through to the product page. A parent who feels the product is premium on screen will trust it enough to buy.
Constraint
Requires coordination with brand partners and photography vendors. Not a single sprint — needs ongoing governance for new product listings.
Trade-off
Choosing in-house visual standards over marketplace aggregation model — requires more curation but produces significantly higher trust signals.
Business Value
Improves Week 1 Retention Rate — % of downloads active more than once in the first week.
2
Must HaveFilter Logic
Persistent Filter State
Fix the filter logic so selected filters persist when a parent moves between sub-categories within the same parent category. A parent who filtered Boy in Party Wear should see Boy applied automatically when switching to Dungarees. Straightforward engineering fix — state management update in the filtering system.
Constraint
Low constraint — this is a logic fix, not a new feature. Should be resolved in a single engineering sprint.
Trade-off
Choosing persistent state over reset-on-navigate — some users may want a fresh filter per category. Add a "Clear filters" button as the escape valve.
Business Value
Reduces friction in browsing flow — directly impacts time-to-cart for working parents with limited session time.
3
Must HaveAI Core Feature
AI Guided Discovery — The Digital Shopkeeper
A warm, illustrated Indian female caricature — modern, relatable, shopkeeper-feel — accessible from any category via a "Help me choose" button. She floats over the current page and asks 4 clarifying questions one at a time: occasion, child's age, gender, and item type (full outfit, top wear, bottom wear, footwear, accessories, or open text). After all 4 answers she opens a dedicated results page showing 2 curated picks pulled from OZi's catalogue ranked by product description match, age and gender tags, and recent purchase trend data. Each recommendation shows a trending signal — upward arrow with number of parents who bought this week. OZi already has an Occasion Wear category — this feature is an intelligence layer on top of existing infrastructure, not a rebuild.
Constraint
AI accuracy depends on catalogue data quality. Risk of irrelevant suggestions if product descriptions are inconsistent. Medical advice guardrail critical for baby essentials category.
Trade-off
Choosing a caricature advisor over a pure chatbot — personality creates trust but requires design investment. Choosing 2 recommendations over more — fewer choices reduce decision fatigue, core to the value proposition.
Business Value
Directly improves AI Conversion Rate — % of AI users who follow the recommendation to cart. Target: 40%+ by Week 6 post-launch.
4
PerformanceFilter UX
Multi-Select Gender Filter
Allow multiple simultaneous selections in the gender filter — Boy + Unisex, or Girl + Unisex — so parents buying for children of different genders or looking for versatile options aren't forced into a binary choice. One of the most underserved parent behaviours in Indian e-commerce: buying for siblings.
Constraint
Low — checkbox UI change on existing filter component. Standard multi-select pattern.
Trade-off
More results with multi-select may reduce curation feel. Pair with sorting by relevance to maintain quality signal.
Business Value
Expands browsing depth for parent segments with multiple children — increases basket size potential.
5
DelighterAI Feature · Phase 2
Virtual Try-On
When a parent opens a fashion product, a "Try on your child" option appears. The app requests a front-facing photo of the child — sitting or standing. The AI overlays the selected outfit on the child's body without altering the face, enhances the smile naturally to reflect the joy of wearing it, and renders the result in 3D with 360-degree rotation. The photo is saved within the session so subsequent try-ons don't require re-uploading — with an option to change the photo anytime. Scoped to fast fashion categories only at launch — essentials, baby care, and gear don't benefit from visual try-on the same way clothing does.
Constraint
AI overlay fidelity risk — distorted child images destroy trust more than no try-on at all. Minimum confidence threshold required before showing result.
Trade-off
Scoped to fashion only — prevents resource depletion before traffic justifies expansion to other categories.
Business Value
Improves product-page-to-cart conversion — reduces the uncertainty that kills purchase confidence at the last step.
06 ICE Scoring & Effort-Impact Matrix

Scoring the
build order.

Impact: how much does this move Week 1 retention and conversion (1–10). Confidence: how sure are we based on evidence (1–10). Ease: how easy for an early-stage team (1–10, 10 = very easy).

Solution KANO Impact Confidence Ease ICE Score
Image QualityMust Have799567
Filter PersistenceMust Have6910540
Multi-select Gender FilterPerformance599405
AI Guided DiscoveryMust Have974252
Virtual Try-OnDelighter65390

Key insight: Image quality and filter fix score highest — low effort, high confidence, immediate impact. Ship these in Sprint 1. AI Guided Discovery has the highest impact score but lowest ease — plan carefully, test AI output quality extensively before launch. KANO overrides ICE: all Must Haves before Performance features.

Do First — High Impact, Low Effort
Image Quality Fix
Filter Persistence Fix
Multi-select Gender Filter
Plan — High Impact, High Effort
AI Guided Discovery
Virtual Try-On (Phase 2)
Fill In
Nothing in this set — all solutions are high-value.
Avoid
Community — low DAU makes it a ghost town. Phase 3.
07 Success Metrics

How you know
it's working.

Formula: Metric name + Baseline + Target + Timeline. If all four can't be filled, the metric isn't ready.

Image + Filter fixes
Week 1 Retention Rate
% of weekly downloads active more than once in their first week. Baseline: established at launch. Foundational fixes directly impact whether a parent returns after first browse.
Target 30%+ · Measured Week 2 post-ship
AI Guided Discovery
AI Discovery Adoption Rate
% of fast fashion category browsers who tap the AI advisor. Baseline: 0 (new feature). Measures whether parents discover and trust the feature enough to use it.
Target 25%+ · Measured Week 4 post-launch
AI Guided Discovery
AI Conversion Rate
% of AI users who follow the recommendation to cart or purchase. Baseline: 0. This is the definitive measure of whether the shopkeeper is doing her job.
Target 40%+ · Measured Week 6 post-launch
Virtual Try-On
Try-On Engagement Rate
% of fashion product page openers who use the virtual try-on feature. Baseline: 0. Measures whether the feature adds to purchase confidence or is ignored.
Target 35%+ · Measured Week 4 post-launch
08 Risks & Mitigations

What could
go wrong.

AI Guided Discovery
AI Accuracy Failure
The guided discovery AI may give incorrect, irrelevant, or poorly ranked suggestions if not trained on OZi's catalogue with sufficient context.
Mitigation
Start with a rule-based layer — hardcode occasion-to-category mappings first, let AI personalise within those guardrails. Test output quality extensively before public launch.
AI Guided Discovery
Medical Advice Risk
In baby essentials categories — skincare, bath products — the AI advisor must never give health or medical recommendations. A guardrail failure here creates legal and trust risk.
Mitigation
Hard restrict AI responses to non-medical domains. If a health query is detected, redirect to "Please consult your paediatrician" — non-negotiable guardrail before launch.
Virtual Try-On
Image Fidelity Failure
AI overlay may distort the child's face or body proportions, or fail to accurately represent the product. A distorted result destroys trust more than no try-on at all.
Mitigation
Set a minimum AI confidence threshold. If below threshold, show "Try-on unavailable for this product" rather than a distorted result. Never show a bad output.
All AI Features
Focus Dilution
In pursuit of building two complex AI features, OZi risks deprioritising foundational fixes — image quality, filter logic — which are cheaper and have more immediate conversion impact.
Mitigation
Ship Must Have fixes in Sprint 1 before any AI feature gets engineering bandwidth. Lock this as product policy — no new feature ships until image and filter bugs are resolved.
09 Live Prototype

We didn't just
describe the fix.
We built it.

A working prototype of the AI Guided Discovery feature — the digital shopkeeper for OZi. All 4 screens, fully designed, showing the exact flow a parent would experience.

Step 01 — Home Page
PM Observation

Home page UX is warm, child-friendly, well-designed. The problem is not here. It begins the moment a parent taps a category.

↓
Step 02 — Category Page · The Problem Moment
The Activation Gap

93 products. No occasion guidance. Filters reset between sub-categories. The caricature advisor peeking from the bottom right is the solution — one tap away.

↓
Step 03 — AI Guided Discovery · 4 Questions
Q1 · Occasion
Q2 · Age
Q3 · Gender
Q4 · Item Type
Design Decision

Not a chatbot. A warm illustrated Indian female character asking 4 questions one at a time with a progress bar. Modal floats over the existing page — parent never loses context.

↓
Step 04 — Results · Curated Picks with Trend Signal
Above fold
With scroll
The Conversion Moment

2 picks — not 93. Each shows "142 parents bought this week." Social proof at peak decision anxiety. Add to Cart is one tap. Done in under 3 minutes.

10 Vision & Roadmap

Where OZi
could go.

Now
0–3 mo
Fix the Foundation
Image quality upgrade, filter persistence fix, multi-select gender filter. These ship in days and immediately improve conversion. Non-negotiable before any AI feature.
Next
3–6 mo
Ship the Shopkeeper
Launch AI Guided Discovery with the caricature advisor. Measure adoption and conversion weekly. Iterate the question flow based on drop-off data. The shopkeeper gets smarter with every parent interaction.
Later
6–12 mo
See It On Your Child
Virtual Try-On when traffic justifies the infrastructure. Community when there are enough repeat buyers to make it feel alive. OZi becomes not just a commerce platform but the trusted parenting companion.

Priya is 29. Product manager at a Bangalore tech firm. Her son turns 2 next Saturday. She has 40 minutes on her lunch break to find something he'll wear to his cousin's birthday party.

She opens OZi. Taps Infant Fashion. A small illustrated character pops up at the bottom right — warm, modern, Indian. "Not sure what to pick? Let me help." Priya taps yes.

Three questions. Occasion: birthday party. Age: 2 years. Gender: boy. One more: full outfit. Thirty seconds.

A new page opens. Two options. Both trending. The first: "142 parents bought this week." The image is crisp. She taps it. Adds to cart. Done in 8 minutes.

That evening she tells her husband: "OZi actually helped me decide."

That word of mouth is worth more than any ad OZi will ever run.
